result:
If I could change the world in one way, I would dismantle the illusion of separation.

It’s the root of so much suffering: the belief that we are fundamentally divided by borders, ideologies, identities, or interests. This separation allows for indifference to others’ pain, the hoarding of resources, and the degradation of our shared environment.

The change would be subtle but deep—a shift in collective consciousness toward recognizing our fundamental interdependence. Not sameness, but interconnectedness. Seeing the health of a stranger, the dignity of someone from a distant culture, the fate of a forest on another continent, as inextricably linked to our own well-being.

This wouldn’t erase conflict or difference, but it would change the ground rules. Empathy wouldn’t be a charitable impulse but a logical response to a shared reality. Competition could evolve into cooperation for a thriving whole.

In practical terms, this shift would reshape everything: economics (from growth-at-all-costs to regenerative systems), politics (from us-vs-them to shared problem-solving), education (from rote memorization to cultivating systemic understanding and compassion), and our relationship with the natural world (from dominion to kinship).

The one change isn’t a law or a technology. It’s the lens through which we see everything else.
